Părinții au fost șocați: „Ce să le zicem vecinilor? Că băiatul nostru lasă linii neterminate? Mai bine le ziceam că merge la teatru. Privind în urmă, semnele […] The post Un tânăr n-a fost lăsat la Beach, Please! de părinți, că anul trecut n-a tras toată linia din farfurie appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
romania.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

medical.title: After the rupture: How communities carry collective trauma and find healing
medical.summary: When tragedy strikes, entire communities can be left reeling. A UNSW researcher says recovery from collective trauma depends on the same force that often causes the harm: the actions of people.

medical.title: New mechanism explains how nerve cells form one long output branch
medical.summary: DZNE researchers have uncovered a mechanism that determines why a neuron usually forms a single, long extension called an "axon"—a phenomenon that is fundamental to how our brain functions. Contrary to the common view that external cues drive axon formation, the team of scientists concluded that its growth originates primarily inside the cell. Their work, based on cell cultures and published in the journal Nature with collaborators from other institutions in Germany, Austria and Japan, reveals how a neuron's structure is remodeled to generate the axon.

medical.title: Sexually transmitted diarrhea spreading rapidly among some networks of gay men in the UK, study finds
medical.summary: Variants of sexually transmitted Shigella—a bacterial infection that causes bloody or prolonged diarrhea and can lead to severe dehydration—are causing more disease and becoming increasingly antibiotic-resistant among sections of the U.K.'s LGBTQ+ community, a new Cambridge study has found.

medical.title: New treatment helps achieve a healthier BMI in people with weight dysregulation after brain tumors
medical.summary: A new therapy helps achieve a healthier BMI in people whose weight has become dysregulated following treatment for a brain tumor, a clinical trial has found. The results of the trial have been published in the New England Journal of Medicine. The new drug, setmelanotide, offers hope for improved health and quality of life for children and adults with hormonal and metabolic dysfunction caused by a brain tumor.

medical.title: Medicare prescriptions for Wegovy jumped after approval for heart disease
medical.summary: Medicare prescriptions for Wegovy increased sharply after the program started covering the anti-obesity medication to prevent heart problems, but just a fraction of beneficiaries likely eligible received the treatment, according to new research published in the American Journal of Managed Care by the USC Schaeffer Center for Health Policy & Economics.

medical.title: Building trust: Relationship between nurses and parents plays vital role in caring for medically fragile infants
medical.summary: Trust between nurses and parents plays a central role in effectively caring for medically fragile infants (MFIs) and supports parental well-being and infant development, according to a new study led by Lyndsay MacKay, an assistant professor in the Texas A&M University College of Nursing. Published in the Journal of Pediatric Nursing, it explores how parents build and maintain trust with nurses while their medically fragile infant is hospitalized for extended periods.

medical.title: Hidden pathway drives COVID-19 infection, triggers damaging inflammation in the lungs
medical.summary: New research has uncovered a hidden pathway that allows COVID-19 to infect the immune system and trigger damaging inflammation in the lungs. The study by La Trobe University and WEHI researchers found SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19, can hide inside tiny fragments of dying infected cells, known as apoptotic bodies (ApoBDs).

medical.title: Evidence reveals that the language of thought is not natural language
medical.summary: Some people find it useful to talk through their problems—but language isn't necessary for logical reasoning, cognitive neuroscientists at MIT's McGovern Institute for Brain Research say.

medical.title: Cognitive and physical limitations can affect inhaler use in COPD
medical.summary: Cognitive function, hand dexterity and breathing ability play an important role in helping people with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) use their inhalers correctly, according to a new study. The study is published in the Annals of the American Thoracic Society.

medical.title: Why some people are more bothered by low-frequency sounds
medical.summary: Some people are more sensitive to low-frequency noise, such as from ventilation systems, heat pumps, wind turbines and transformers. Why is that?

medical.title: Researchers hone new AI method to track 'smart' vapes with digital screens
medical.summary: E-cigarettes, also known as vapes, are battery-operated devices that heat a liquid that typically contains nicotine, an addictive substance. These devices are continually changing, with new flavors, novel device designs and digital screens. Some of these e-cigarettes—sometimes called "smart vapes"—include built-in games and Bluetooth connectivity that have the potential to gamify the use of nicotine. Many of these devices are marketed online but cannot be easily monitored with existing data sources and methods.

medical.title: AI can't replace mental health therapists. But here's where it might make a difference
medical.summary: A person wakes in the middle of the night, overwhelmed and needing someone to talk to. But instead of calling a loved one or booking a counseling session, they open ChatGPT.

medical.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
medical.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

medical.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

medical.title: Scientists discover hidden “footprints of death” that may help viruses spread
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new twist in what happens when cells die. As dying cells break apart, they leave behind tiny “footprints of death” packed with newly discovered particles that help guide the immune system to clean up the remains. But researchers found that influenza viruses can exploit this process, hiding inside these microscopic packages and potentially using them to spread to nearby cells.

medical.title: Study challenges a common belief about vitamin D and sunlight
medical.summary: A study of nearly 300 people across northern Britain found that vitamin D levels often stay low all year in groups most at risk. Surprisingly, summer sunshine did not significantly boost vitamin D levels among older adults or people from minoritized ethnic backgrounds.

science.title: Ancient 100-kilometer Himalayan glacier once reached lower than many of India's famous hill stations
science.summary: A new study published in Quaternary Science Reviews dates the dramatic collapse of one of the largest glaciers ever documented in the Himalayas. The findings overturn a long-held assumption about what sustains wet-climate (monsoon-dominated) glaciers.

science.title: One in four managers withholds feedback from those they supervise, even when the news is positive
science.summary: Performance feedback is critical for supporting career and education decisions, but in a new study published in Management Science, a research team from the University of Portsmouth, the University of Exeter and York University uncovered a striking pattern of managers partially or fully withholding performance feedback that was particularly pronounced among female workers.

science.title: Curiosity Sees Martian Sulfur Up Close
science.summary: This close-up view shows fragments of sulfur crystals — the first ever seen on the Red Planet. The crystals were found after NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover happened to drive over a rock and crush it on May 30, 2024. Several days later, Curiosity used a camera on the end of its robotic arm to take […]

science.title: Seagrass meadows could help nourish millions, new study finds
science.summary: Seagrass meadows play a largely overlooked role in providing nutrition for coastal communities, a new study published in Cell Reports Sustainability has found. The research, led by scientists at Project Seagrass and Stockholm University, found that fish living in seagrass meadows can provide a richer mix of essential nutrients than fish living on nearby coral reefs.

science.title: New technique takes the heat out of 3D printing process
science.summary: Researchers have developed a new 3D printing technique that allows the printing of whole objects while controlling the temperature of the chemical reaction to stabilize the process. Academics in the University of Nottingham's Faculty of Engineering, in collaboration with the University of California, Berkeley, have developed an enhancement for a type of 3D printing called Volumetric Additive Manufacturing (VAM), which can create whole objects in seconds to minutes. The research has been published in Nature Communications.

science.title: The ghost in Orion's shell: Hydrogen maps show repeated stellar feedback sculpted around Orion Nebula
science.summary: An international team led by Juan Diego Soler at the University of Vienna used two of the world's most powerful radio telescopes to uncover previously hidden structures within the Orion Nebula. The project produced the sharpest maps ever made of neutral hydrogen in that region of massive star formation. The findings expose the complex relationship of star-forming regions with their environment and suggest that the Orion Nebula has been shaped by multiple episodes of stellar feedback rather than a single expanding bubble. The study has been published in the journal Astronomy & Astrophysics.

science.title: Mild enzymatic method gently refines algae oil for nutrition products
science.summary: Algae oil is increasingly used as a sustainable source of important nutrients, including polyunsaturated fatty acids that support human health and development. One important example is arachidonic acid, or ARA, which is used in nutrition products such as infant formula and functional foods.

science.title: Unraveling the glass-like nature of epithelial tissues
science.summary: In a new study, researchers at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) have resolved a longstanding mystery by showing how epithelial tissues exhibit slow-moving, glass-like behavior despite their fast-paced biological activity. Their study is published in the journal Nature Communications.

science.title: Newborn stars preserve organic-rich gas within ancient supernova debris
science.summary: For the first time, astronomers have discovered stellar cocoons rich in complex organic molecules within a supernova remnant. A research team from Niigata University, Gifu University, RIKEN and Kyoto University in Japan used the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) to observe the remnant of a massive star that exploded about 1,600 years ago.

science.title: NASA Scientists Take to Air and Space to Study Arctic Sea Ice
science.summary: This month, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California are testing a spacecraft sensor that will help measure how quickly Arctic sea ice is disappearing. And while that instrument won’t launch for another year, scientists started preparing for its use during a recent field campaign in the Canadian wilderness. Researchers spent two weeks […]

science.title: Honesty may be more efficient than incentives in organizations, new research finds
science.summary: For decades, economic theory has often treated people as if they will do the right thing in organizations only when incentives, such as performance pay, force them to. But does this miss the fact that many people also care about honesty?

science.title: Fish DNA and 10,000 crystals rewrite Colorado River's Grand Canyon origin story
science.summary: For more than 150 years, scientists have debated when and how the Colorado River first carved its way through the Grand Canyon. Now, a new study led by researchers at the University of New Mexico offers evidence that the river developed gradually from north to south between 8 million and 4.8 million years ago.

science.title: Next‑generation membranes can refine crude oil using under half the energy of distillation
science.summary: Oil refining is necessary for transforming raw, unusable crude oil into valuable goods like gasoline, diesel, jet fuel and petrochemical feedstocks. However, the usual distillation process is energy-intensive, spurring researchers to find better, more efficient ways of refining oil. A new study published in Science describes a potential solution to this problem in the form of a specialized membrane. So far, these membranes are proving to be a scalable and highly plausible industrial technology, and testing has shown promising results for significantly reducing the energy needs of oil processing.

science.title: Pregnant women may avoid child protection out of fear and mistrust
science.summary: Pregnant women who become involved with child protection services often experience fear, mistrust and stigma, leading some to avoid health and support services altogether, new Griffith University research has found. Ph.D. candidate Rebecca Wren from Griffith's School of Allied Health, Sport and Social Work examined existing research and what it revealed about the lived experience of women who engaged with child protection while pregnant. The findings are published in the journal Children and Youth Services Review.

science.title: Using mechanical vibrations instead of magnetic memory for quantum computing
science.summary: Quantum computers still face limits when it comes to storing information. Researchers at ETH Zurich are now turning to mechanical vibrations rather than electromagnetic memory. Their new vibrating memory can store significantly more information in a smaller volume. Combined with a suitable computer architecture, it also enables the efficient solution of complex computational problems.

science.title: El Nino powers up as forecasters predict historic strength and a rainier winter for the US South
science.summary: An intensifying El Niño, nature's heat-releasing thermostat that spikes global temperatures, is heading to historically strong levels, the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration said Thursday.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: O soluție tehnologică pentru mobilitate urbană sustenabilă a primit premiul Best Tech Idea în cadrul finalei naționale JA Gen-E 2026

România a acordat premiul Best Tech Idea echipei MOVIO, formată din studenți ai Universității… Articolul O soluție tehnologică pentru mobilitate urbană sustenabilă a primit premiul Best Tech Idea în cadrul finalei naționale JA Gen-E 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Echipa de curatori ai FNT 2026, alcătuită din criticii de teatru Raluca Cîrciumaru, Alina Epîngeac și Sebastian-Vlad Popa, a inclus spectacolul Teatrului Național în această ediție a festivalului, ediție al… Articolul Teatrul Național din Timișoara este deosebit de încântat să anunțe că spectacolul CÂNTĂREAȚA CHEALĂ de Eugène Ionesco, regia artistică Mihai Măniuțiu a fost selecționat în Festivalul Național de Teatru 2026. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Un tânăr n-a fost lăsat la Beach, Please! de părinți, că anul trecut n-a tras toată linia din farfurie
mix_items.summary: Un tânăr din România nu a mai fost lăsat de părinți la Beach, Please!, după ce anul trecut a comis rușinea familiei: n-a tras toată linia din farfurie. Părinții au fost șocați: „Ce să le zicem vecinilor? Că băiatul nostru lasă linii neterminate? Mai bine le ziceam că merge la teatru. Privind în urmă, semnele […] The post Un tânăr n-a fost lăsat la Beach, Please! de părinți, că anul trecut n-a tras toată linia din farfurie appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Când referința nu mai e legea, ci „ ce vrea p*la mea”, așa cum e cazul sentințelor date de Tribunalul București, doar un naiv ar putea să fie surprins de ultima decizie a Tribunalului București. Doar cineva care nu a trăit în România ultimilor ani ar putea fi uimit de faptul că Tribunalul București tocmai […] The post Tribunalul București a decis că toate deciziile Tribunalului București sunt nule appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: After the rupture: How communities carry collective trauma and find healing
mix_items.summary: When tragedy strikes, entire communities can be left reeling. A UNSW researcher says recovery from collective trauma depends on the same force that often causes the harm: the actions of people.

mix_items.title: New mechanism explains how nerve cells form one long output branch
mix_items.summary: DZNE researchers have uncovered a mechanism that determines why a neuron usually forms a single, long extension called an "axon"—a phenomenon that is fundamental to how our brain functions. Contrary to the common view that external cues drive axon formation, the team of scientists concluded that its growth originates primarily inside the cell. Their work, based on cell cultures and published in the journal Nature with collaborators from other institutions in Germany, Austria and Japan, reveals how a neuron's structure is remodeled to generate the axon.

mix_items.title: Ancient 100-kilometer Himalayan glacier once reached lower than many of India's famous hill stations
mix_items.summary: A new study published in Quaternary Science Reviews dates the dramatic collapse of one of the largest glaciers ever documented in the Himalayas. The findings overturn a long-held assumption about what sustains wet-climate (monsoon-dominated) glaciers.

mix_items.title: One in four managers withholds feedback from those they supervise, even when the news is positive
mix_items.summary: Performance feedback is critical for supporting career and education decisions, but in a new study published in Management Science, a research team from the University of Portsmouth, the University of Exeter and York University uncovered a striking pattern of managers partially or fully withholding performance feedback that was particularly pronounced among female workers.

mix_items.title: Sexually transmitted diarrhea spreading rapidly among some networks of gay men in the UK, study finds
mix_items.summary: Variants of sexually transmitted Shigella—a bacterial infection that causes bloody or prolonged diarrhea and can lead to severe dehydration—are causing more disease and becoming increasingly antibiotic-resistant among sections of the U.K.'s LGBTQ+ community, a new Cambridge study has found.

mix_items.title: Curiosity Sees Martian Sulfur Up Close
mix_items.summary: This close-up view shows fragments of sulfur crystals — the first ever seen on the Red Planet. The crystals were found after NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover happened to drive over a rock and crush it on May 30, 2024. Several days later, Curiosity used a camera on the end of its robotic arm to take […]

mix_items.title: Seagrass meadows could help nourish millions, new study finds
mix_items.summary: Seagrass meadows play a largely overlooked role in providing nutrition for coastal communities, a new study published in Cell Reports Sustainability has found. The research, led by scientists at Project Seagrass and Stockholm University, found that fish living in seagrass meadows can provide a richer mix of essential nutrients than fish living on nearby coral reefs.

mix_items.title: New technique takes the heat out of 3D printing process
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed a new 3D printing technique that allows the printing of whole objects while controlling the temperature of the chemical reaction to stabilize the process. Academics in the University of Nottingham's Faculty of Engineering, in collaboration with the University of California, Berkeley, have developed an enhancement for a type of 3D printing called Volumetric Additive Manufacturing (VAM), which can create whole objects in seconds to minutes. The research has been published in Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: The ghost in Orion's shell: Hydrogen maps show repeated stellar feedback sculpted around Orion Nebula
mix_items.summary: An international team led by Juan Diego Soler at the University of Vienna used two of the world's most powerful radio telescopes to uncover previously hidden structures within the Orion Nebula. The project produced the sharpest maps ever made of neutral hydrogen in that region of massive star formation. The findings expose the complex relationship of star-forming regions with their environment and suggest that the Orion Nebula has been shaped by multiple episodes of stellar feedback rather than a single expanding bubble. The study has been published in the journal Astronomy & Astrophysics.

mix_items.title: New treatment helps achieve a healthier BMI in people with weight dysregulation after brain tumors
mix_items.summary: A new therapy helps achieve a healthier BMI in people whose weight has become dysregulated following treatment for a brain tumor, a clinical trial has found. The results of the trial have been published in the New England Journal of Medicine. The new drug, setmelanotide, offers hope for improved health and quality of life for children and adults with hormonal and metabolic dysfunction caused by a brain tumor.

mix_items.title: Mild enzymatic method gently refines algae oil for nutrition products
mix_items.summary: Algae oil is increasingly used as a sustainable source of important nutrients, including polyunsaturated fatty acids that support human health and development. One important example is arachidonic acid, or ARA, which is used in nutrition products such as infant formula and functional foods.

mix_items.title: Unraveling the glass-like nature of epithelial tissues
mix_items.summary: In a new study, researchers at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) have resolved a longstanding mystery by showing how epithelial tissues exhibit slow-moving, glass-like behavior despite their fast-paced biological activity. Their study is published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Medicare prescriptions for Wegovy jumped after approval for heart disease
mix_items.summary: Medicare prescriptions for Wegovy increased sharply after the program started covering the anti-obesity medication to prevent heart problems, but just a fraction of beneficiaries likely eligible received the treatment, according to new research published in the American Journal of Managed Care by the USC Schaeffer Center for Health Policy & Economics.

mix_items.title: Newborn stars preserve organic-rich gas within ancient supernova debris
mix_items.summary: For the first time, astronomers have discovered stellar cocoons rich in complex organic molecules within a supernova remnant. A research team from Niigata University, Gifu University, RIKEN and Kyoto University in Japan used the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) to observe the remnant of a massive star that exploded about 1,600 years ago.

mix_items.title: NASA Scientists Take to Air and Space to Study Arctic Sea Ice
mix_items.summary: This month, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California are testing a spacecraft sensor that will help measure how quickly Arctic sea ice is disappearing. And while that instrument won’t launch for another year, scientists started preparing for its use during a recent field campaign in the Canadian wilderness. Researchers spent two weeks […]

mix_items.title: Honesty may be more efficient than incentives in organizations, new research finds
mix_items.summary: For decades, economic theory has often treated people as if they will do the right thing in organizations only when incentives, such as performance pay, force them to. But does this miss the fact that many people also care about honesty?

mix_items.title: Building trust: Relationship between nurses and parents plays vital role in caring for medically fragile infants
mix_items.summary: Trust between nurses and parents plays a central role in effectively caring for medically fragile infants (MFIs) and supports parental well-being and infant development, according to a new study led by Lyndsay MacKay, an assistant professor in the Texas A&M University College of Nursing. Published in the Journal of Pediatric Nursing, it explores how parents build and maintain trust with nurses while their medically fragile infant is hospitalized for extended periods.

mix_items.title: Hidden pathway drives COVID-19 infection, triggers damaging inflammation in the lungs
mix_items.summary: New research has uncovered a hidden pathway that allows COVID-19 to infect the immune system and trigger damaging inflammation in the lungs. The study by La Trobe University and WEHI researchers found SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19, can hide inside tiny fragments of dying infected cells, known as apoptotic bodies (ApoBDs).

mix_items.title: Fish DNA and 10,000 crystals rewrite Colorado River's Grand Canyon origin story
mix_items.summary: For more than 150 years, scientists have debated when and how the Colorado River first carved its way through the Grand Canyon. Now, a new study led by researchers at the University of New Mexico offers evidence that the river developed gradually from north to south between 8 million and 4.8 million years ago.

mix_items.title: Evidence reveals that the language of thought is not natural language
mix_items.summary: Some people find it useful to talk through their problems—but language isn't necessary for logical reasoning, cognitive neuroscientists at MIT's McGovern Institute for Brain Research say.

mix_items.title: Next‑generation membranes can refine crude oil using under half the energy of distillation
mix_items.summary: Oil refining is necessary for transforming raw, unusable crude oil into valuable goods like gasoline, diesel, jet fuel and petrochemical feedstocks. However, the usual distillation process is energy-intensive, spurring researchers to find better, more efficient ways of refining oil. A new study published in Science describes a potential solution to this problem in the form of a specialized membrane. So far, these membranes are proving to be a scalable and highly plausible industrial technology, and testing has shown promising results for significantly reducing the energy needs of oil processing.

mix_items.title: Cognitive and physical limitations can affect inhaler use in COPD
mix_items.summary: Cognitive function, hand dexterity and breathing ability play an important role in helping people with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) use their inhalers correctly, according to a new study. The study is published in the Annals of the American Thoracic Society.

mix_items.title: Pregnant women may avoid child protection out of fear and mistrust
mix_items.summary: Pregnant women who become involved with child protection services often experience fear, mistrust and stigma, leading some to avoid health and support services altogether, new Griffith University research has found. Ph.D. candidate Rebecca Wren from Griffith's School of Allied Health, Sport and Social Work examined existing research and what it revealed about the lived experience of women who engaged with child protection while pregnant. The findings are published in the journal Children and Youth Services Review.

mix_items.title: Why some people are more bothered by low-frequency sounds
mix_items.summary: Some people are more sensitive to low-frequency noise, such as from ventilation systems, heat pumps, wind turbines and transformers. Why is that?

mix_items.title: Researchers hone new AI method to track 'smart' vapes with digital screens
mix_items.summary: E-cigarettes, also known as vapes, are battery-operated devices that heat a liquid that typically contains nicotine, an addictive substance. These devices are continually changing, with new flavors, novel device designs and digital screens. Some of these e-cigarettes—sometimes called "smart vapes"—include built-in games and Bluetooth connectivity that have the potential to gamify the use of nicotine. Many of these devices are marketed online but cannot be easily monitored with existing data sources and methods.

mix_items.title: AI can't replace mental health therapists. But here's where it might make a difference
mix_items.summary: A person wakes in the middle of the night, overwhelmed and needing someone to talk to. But instead of calling a loved one or booking a counseling session, they open ChatGPT.

mix_items.title: Using mechanical vibrations instead of magnetic memory for quantum computing
mix_items.summary: Quantum computers still face limits when it comes to storing information. Researchers at ETH Zurich are now turning to mechanical vibrations rather than electromagnetic memory. Their new vibrating memory can store significantly more information in a smaller volume. Combined with a suitable computer architecture, it also enables the efficient solution of complex computational problems.

mix_items.title: El Nino powers up as forecasters predict historic strength and a rainier winter for the US South
mix_items.summary: An intensifying El Niño, nature's heat-releasing thermostat that spikes global temperatures, is heading to historically strong levels, the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration said Thursday.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
mix_items.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden “footprints of death” that may help viruses spread
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new twist in what happens when cells die. As dying cells break apart, they leave behind tiny “footprints of death” packed with newly discovered particles that help guide the immune system to clean up the remains. But researchers found that influenza viruses can exploit this process, hiding inside these microscopic packages and potentially using them to spread to nearby cells.

mix_items.title: Study challenges a common belief about vitamin D and sunlight
mix_items.summary: A study of nearly 300 people across northern Britain found that vitamin D levels often stay low all year in groups most at risk. Surprisingly, summer sunshine did not significantly boost vitamin D levels among older adults or people from minoritized ethnic backgrounds.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Ce faci diseara? - Ma odihnesc de la cat n-am facut azi.

satire_ro.title: Pistoale cadou de la Erdogan pentru toţi liderii NATO. Nicuşor Dan a primit unul cu apă
